course_description: This course introduces innovation as a capability to create and sustain an innovation culture. An innovation capability helps startups to retain their creative approaches to emerging opportunities, SME's looking to scale their innovation processes and larger organisations that lack or seek to consolidate a systematic innovation capability. Students learn how to identify, build and lead innovation capabilities and apply this knowledge in problem-solving teams in live settings.

requirements.assumed_background: This course is offered to postgraduate students who ideally have undertaken some study in entrepreneurship & innovation or with some practical experience in running innovation-based enterprises. While no pre-requisite courses are set for EIBS7303, it is assumed students have an interest in, or aspiration to become engaged in, the process of building innovation capabilities within new ventures or already established firms.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Time will be allocated for teams to develop & expand their team presentations & case study project within the scheduled class timetable and to align this development with course materials. Further, the learning activities include a series of interactions with a new venture founder to provide information of the real-time case and to respond to issues that emerge in students completing the assessment task. Students are encouraged to make best use of these timetabled sessions together as they represent a rich and irreplaceable problem-solving resource.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The central foundation for this course is the building of a case study based on a live entrepreneurial venture, interpreting the viable & beneficial pathways for embedding an innovation capability into that venture in real-time, while gaining information, advice and feedback from the venture founder. The study will be interpreted through team-based presentations and a written study of the case. In addition, students will enhance skill development through a series of quiz sets that focus on the theoretical foundations of innovation building and keep a Research Journal that tracks their personal evolution in undertaking this engaged scholarship process.

learning_outcomes.description: evaluate the foundations of innovation performance in balancing flexibility with efficiency in dynamic environments
interactively present evaluation outputs in a compelling way to guide future capability decisions
identify a personal portfolio of attributes that can be used to build future innovation capabilities
Understand the challenges of building innovation capabilities and embedding them in an organization

assessment_summary.title: Building Innovation Capability*
assessment_summary.due_date: 19 Oct 18 16:00; Upload ONE report per team

assessment_details.title: 5 quiz sets | released after sessions 1,2,4,5,7
assessment_details.due_date: 29 Jul 18 16:00 - 09 Sep 18 16:00; Each quiz open for ~6 days
assessment_details.format: Short answer, Short essay, Extended essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The 5 quiz sets each offer a distillation of key foundational theory and their application to real-world cases, so to develop analytic & descriptive skills in building innovation capabilities. Each quiz is completed individually by each student throughout the semester (allowing for breaks & team presentations), testing your knowledge in different ways, to develop applied understanding of the range of innovation content covered across the course. Each quiz attracts up to 4 marks, with 20 marks total available for this individual assessment item, with a quiz released at the close of each of sessions 1, 2, 4, 5 & 7 and due prior to the next session.

In addition, each quiz will invite your individual feedback on any aspect of the preceding week's seminar that requires more explanation, to be covered subsequently by way of clarification and expansion or by individual response through email, as required. Your response to each of these feedback questions attract NIL marks and are intended to give you a regular opportunity to raise any content concerns.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: Case Framing* & Innovation Pathways*
assessment_details.task_description:
To undertake this study, the class is divided into teams with each team interpreting the available data and applying their insights as derived from innovation theory to the focal case. In response to the data provided by the venture founder, teams will also get to pitch their innovation capability ideas to the founder, seeking to derive & apply novel insights whereby innovation & capabilities theory can assist the startup venture. Teams will make TWO presentations to the founder as they each build their own case study and each presentation forms part of the team assessment constituting a total of 20 marks, with each team score moderated by the peer assessment factor (PAF), to yield an overall individual score for each item.

Presentation 1 - Framing the Case (10 marks - Session 6 on 27 Aug 2018)

Teams present their outline of the data collected to date and an outline framework for building their case study, so to capture key innovation capability insights as the venture pathway emerges. Since the case is forming in real time, the presentation needs to allow for future events, both promising and adverse, that may impact enactment of innovation options.

Presentation 2 - Innovation Pathways (10 marks - Session 10 on 8 Oct 2018)

The second presentation requires each team to present the pathways to embedding innovation into the emerging venture, drawing on events as these have unfolded in real time and projecting promising growth trajectories, possible adverse conditions and key assumptions underpinning both. The aim is to advise the founder as to practical steps that can be taken to build innovation capability.

assessment_details.title: Building Innovation Capability*
assessment_details.due_date: 19 Oct 18 16:00; Upload ONE report per team
assessment_details.task_description: The team project concludes with a written case study that appraises the history of the focal case and the pathways available to it in building innovation capabilities across a range of assumption scenarios. The case study seeks to capture the venture history, innovations inherent in its formation, how a capability to embed innovation into its future can be built and the theoretical frameworks that assist with analysing & interpreting such future growth, both under promising and adverse conditions. Because the venture is being studied in real time and continues to navigate many uncertainties, the case study should present at least TWO innovation development scenarios, distinguished by clear assumptions & environmental contexts, mapping criteria and implications for future research and practice.

learning_resources.required_resources:
The readings, cases, and online resources for each week can be found in the Learning Resources section of Blackboard. Lecture Recordings, capturing the audio/visual of each seminar, are also gathered in this Learning Resources section for review as required.

http://learn.uq.edu.au
learning_resources.recommended_resources: While various published texts will be referenced in delivering this course, no specific text is required for student absorption. Journal papers that may be given as readings as well as topical case data will be provided to students through the Learning Resources section of Blackboard. Similarly, a discussion board will also be available via Blackboard or as directed by the Course Co-ordinator.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/EIBS7303 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Since students in this subject will be undertaking an engaged scholarship program with a live, new venture founder, each student will be asked to sign a Mutual Confidentiality Agreement at the start of the course, indicating their respect for the privileged materials that will be shared and in recognition of the significance of non-disclosure of such information outside the class.

Further, the journal articles and case studies supplied to you are licenced specifically for use in EIBS7303 during Semester 2, 2018 in support of your study as a student officially enrolled in the course and registered as such in Si-Net. For many of the articles and cases that are provided, you may not be able to gain access to those files after this semester. Your observance of this obligation to only use these materials for EIBS7303 and the course of your studies is assumed by your accepting enrolment. Your observance also supports the royalty agreements held by UQ with various publishers and postgraduate course providers around the world.

Once this course is completed, the licence for journal articles expires and so you may not access these items in accordance with the licence agreement. However, your obligations under the Mutual Confidentiality Agreement endure for a period of five (5) years.

Learn (Blackboard)

All courses are supported by the UQ Learning Management System, Blackboard Learn, which provides an interactive environment for the students and teachers. This is accessible via PC or Mac or Mobile (Apps) environment.

To log on to Blackboard, go to https://learn.uq.edu.au.

As Blackboard is a web‐based application, you will need a recently‐released browser for your computer. For the latest Release Notes for Bb Student, see http://en-gb.help.blackboard.com/Bb_Student/z_Release_Notes_Bb_Student .

For help with Learn.UQ (Blackboard), see https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/learnuq-blackboard-contacts or contact AskUs https://web.library.uq.edu.au/contact-us or telephone 334 64312.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: S1 | Course Introduction (Seminar): Resources, routine actions, heuristics, capabilities, innovation and competitive advantage. Course assessment architecture Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard

S2 | Threshold of Stability (Seminar): Innovation and operating stability, how uncertainty impacts technical & market innovation, navigating promising & adverse conditions Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard

S3 | Flightsuit (Case Study): Venture history from inception, technology & market gaps, lean innovation activities, driving growth. Session co-presented with Scott Hutchens (CEO - Flightsuit) Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard
S4 | Engaged Scholarship (Seminar): How research and practice require engagement to produce new knowledge, change management & innovation strategy Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard

S5 | Innovation & Strategic Opportunities (Seminar): Harnessing capabilities to reduce uncertainty, enabling technical & market innovations to bring strategic options into executable focus Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard
S6 | Framing the Case (Presentation): Present an Outline of how you intend to build your case study, to confirm your framing of the problems and structure for ongoing innovation pathways

S7 | Foundations for Performance (Seminar): Techniques for balancing efficiency with flexibility as the firm grows beyond startup and lays down routine actions for sustaining an innovation culture Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard
S8 | Structuring for Innovation (Seminar): Interlacing identity with strategy, culture and innovation portfolio planning Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard

S9 | Innovation Leadership (Case Study): Framework for new product development to embed innovation into the foundations of the growth of the firm with lasting beneficial impact Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard
Mid Semester Break (No classes 24Sep nor 1Oct): Collaborate in your teams to prepare for your Innovation Pathways pitch in S10 and your case write-up. Also continue building your Research Journal
S10 | Innovation Pathways (Presentation): Present the key innovation elements for the focal case to other teams and the founder (as available)

S11 | Diagnosis of innovation capabilities (Seminar): Applying tools to understand where gaps exist and determining what actions may resolve these gaps in innovation capability; entrepreneurial energy as an innovation driver Readings/Ref: As supplied; Blackboard

S12 | Building Innovation Capability (Discussion): In-class wrap with case founder (as available), reviewing how innovation can be embedded from startup as well as retro-fitting innovation into already established firms. Review Research Journal completion Readings/Ref: As supplied

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Feedback in this Course

The Student Evaluation of Course and Teacher (SECaT) undertaken late in the semester is a vital feedback mechanism for this course, recording your experiences of the relevance & impact of the course content & delivery and directly impacting & improving future offerings of EIBS7303 . While SECaT is a voluntary survey, it is made available to all students enrolled in this course and your feedback is greatly valued as it helps to enhance future course deliveries and program structures within the UQ Business School.

In addition, each of the 5 quiz sets invite feedback as to specific content issues that can then be addressed during later seminars or by direct response to the student. Both forms of such feedback enhance the teaching impact of the course and your engagement with this mechanism is strongly encouraged.

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

School of Business Assessment Guidelines

Student Notices are posted progressively on the UQ Business School website http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student_notices

Reviewing Assessment

The Assessment Viewing Register procedures are:

Go to Current Student – Student Notices – Exam Viewing – online form here then log in.

Students should log in using their normal UQ name (e.g. s9990000) and password.

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment

Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment are not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers.

The sessions are scheduled during weeks 1 and 2 of the teaching week in both semester one and two; and in the case of summer examinations during weeks 1 and 2 in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands.

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation.

Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results

Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the Course Coordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the Course Coordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Associate Dean Academic of the BEL Faculty.

Re-marks

A student who believes the judgement of their work and the resulting mark or grade they were awarded for a piece of assessment does not reflect his/her performance as measured against the published assessment criteria, can apply for a re-mark.

The result of re-marking should not unfairly disadvantage other students.

A re-mark will be conducted once only on the piece of assessment.

In all cases, a re-mark will replace the original mark for the piece of assessment in the calculation of the final grade which could result in a higher or lower grade.

Grounds for consideration

An application for a re-mark is considered only when the student:

has sought and received feedback about their performance on the assessment from the course coordinator or lecturer concerned (or has viewed the piece of assessment and, where available, model answers/written comments or other feedback/etc); and

after receiving feedback, provides a sound written academic case to demonstrate how the mark or grade awarded for an entire task, or a discrete component of a multi-component task, does not reflect their performance against the advertised criteria and standards for that work. Students should reference the published assessment criteria for the piece of assessment and clearly show where they believe there are grounds for reconsideration of the assessment judgement.

The desire for extra marks to achieve a higher final course grade is not sufficient grounds for a re-mark.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

Computing Facilities

Courses offered by UQ Business School utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia.

Professional Code of Conduct

The University of Queensland has strict guidelines around academic integrity, plagiarism, and assessment which should be carefully read in the policy sections of this eCP. In addition, UQ Business School expects that as future business leaders and entrepreneurs, our students will adhere to the highest standards of ethical behaviour in all of their coursework.

Attendance at lectures, tutorials, seminars and workshops is expected and students are reminded that lecture recordings are not designed to replace in-class interactions and learning experiences.

It is a common courtesy to your fellow students and teaching staff to ensure you are on time for all class sessions, that you come prepared and are ready and willing to actively participate in class activities. Leaving class early is also disruptive to your fellow students and lecturers/tutors.

Please use professional etiquette and courtesy in all of your written and oral communication with teaching staff and fellow students. Please also ensure that all written correspondence includes your name and student ID.

Another matter of etiquette is the use of phones, laptops and other electronic devices in class. Some in-class activities may require the use of devices but otherwise we expect that these will be switched off during class time.

Students are not to make their own recordings of lectures or tutorials as this contravenes the UQ policy on Student Integrity and Misconduct. This includes taking photos of lecture or tutorial slides and/or other materials. Teaching staff will distribute all required materials to you via Blackboard.

Course Coordinators and lecturers may set additional requirements and expectations for their respective courses.

Other Course Guidelines

EIBS7303 aims to weave innovation into your participation in an engaged scholarship context, so you can live the experience of capability building within a real-world evolving venture. To this end, parts of some seminar sessions will be captured on video for inclusion in this or related learning activities in the current or subsequent semesters and to capture data on the use of real-time case study in building innovation pedagogy. Any individuals whose faces are recognisable may be asked to sign a Videography Consent Form, depending on the uses for the video. Advance notice will be given prior to sessions where video capture will occur.

Student experiences form a valuable component in understanding such activity, both for the individual student and the class cohort. In addition, the experiences and venture formation information shared by the founder of the focal case forms the foundation of our learning and innovation capability development. Class discussions in each session of the course are assumed to occur in confidence (that is, not to be repeated or discussed with others outside the context of the class, without express permission), to encourage respectful and open dialogue, free exchange of ideas and unrestricted appraisal of innovation capabilities and their application across an array of scenarios. It is an expectation of the UQ Business School that this confidence & respect is observed throughout the course, even extending to ethical obligations beyond the course teaching period and the semester. To this end, each student will be required to sign a Mutual Confidentiality Agreement in recognition of these non-disclosure obligations surrounding the new venture case, which endures for five (5) years from date of signing.

Any concerns or questions relating to these guidelines above should be addressed to the Course Co-ordinator.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
The course explores how innovation can be embedded into both new ventures and also established firms. EIBS7303 inter-twines with a live case of a recently formed startup, allowing students to both observe in
real-time how innovation is enacted in an emerging venture AND also to influence how innovation is embedded as a capability into that venture so to deliver sustained competitive advantages. This
engaged scholarship drives an iterative cycle of problem formulation, theory building, research design and problem solving that ultimately builds theoretical & practical insights.

The course culminates its skill building with the creation of a Case Study , highlighting how innovation capabilities have been built while outlining the steps that venture can take so to
consolidate ongoing innovation as it navigates both promising & adverse conditions. It also requires the preparation of a personal Research Journal that captures incorporation of learning into
future research and practice.

The course includes current trends in capabilities, heuristics, innovation, new venture growth and organisational resilience and requires you to:

read and prepare materials in advance for active class participation;

respect the confidentiality & privileges of the real-time information shared with the founder of the focal case study;

build innovation & capability analysis skills by responding to a series of quiz sets;

present the outputs of such analysis to the focal venture foundertowards building an instructive case study of that venture as it navigates early stage issues; and

expand your skills by exploring real-world opportunities and teh assumptions underpinning progress towards sustained competitive advantage.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

This is a new course offered for Semester 2, 2018 and is interlaced with EIBS7302 Evidence-based Innovation being offered concurrently. Your feedback at the end of semester will form a vital component in improving the course for future students.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

other_learning_activities_information:
Weekly Seminars

In addition to students' own independent study, the main vehicle for course delivery is a weekly 3 hour seminar that will provide instructor delivery, case discussion and team workshop opportunities as well as enabling presentations of team assessment items. EIBS7303 aims to weave innovation into your participation in an engaged scholarship context, so you can live the experience of capability building within an evolving venture. To this end, parts of some seminar sessions will be captured on video for inclusion in this or related learning activities in the current or subsequent semesters and to capture data on the use of case study in building innovation pedagogy. Any individuals whose faces are recognisable may be required to sign a Videography Consent Form , depending on the uses for the video. Advance notice will be given prior to sessions where video capture will occur.

Blackboard

The University's elearning facility, Blackboard, is enabled for this course. Students will have access to: 1) weekly powerpoint slides; 2) course announcements; 3) recording of seminars already presented; 4) Learning Resources to support seminars; and 5) an electronic link to the course profile.

Consultation

Teaching staff will always seek to assist you and can be approached after each seminar. The Course Co-ordinator, Dr Russell Manfield, will also field enquiries in the first instance by email to: r.manfield@business.uq.edu.au after which follow on consultations may be scheduled as appropriate.

Administration

All content related questions and specific questions re the administration of this course should be directed to the Course Co-ordinator, Dr Russell Manfield. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School School Reception and Student Administration: Level 2 Colin Clark Building #39, St Lucia Campus.

Student Responsibility

It is your responsibility to attend classes and consult Blackboard for EIBS7303 as well as designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including assessment submission deadlines and examination times. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

-

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

-

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

-

Other Requirements & Comments:

Grades will be allocated according to University-wide standards of criterion Based Assessment.

late_submission:
Students must fill in an Application for Extension of Assessment Due Date and provide supporting documentation to Assessment, Examinations & Misconducts Coordinator https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension at least 24 hours prior to the submission date.

Your application must be submitted at the location and by the due date specified in Section 5.3 of the relevant Electronic Course Profile (ECP).

Supporting documentation such as a medical certificate, funeral notice etc. must be provided. Scanned or photographed copies should be attached to your email.

For an application on medical grounds, the medical practitioner must not be a near relative or close associate. Examples of near relatives are partner, child, brother, sister, parent. Examples of close associates are close friends, neighbours and partners or children of colleagues.

Extension criteria are applied consistently for equity reasons.

You may discuss your situation with your course coordinator, but you still need to make a formal extension request using this form.

Applications on medical grounds will be approved for the number of calendar days the medical certificate indicates you were unfit for study. You are expected to act in a timely manner and must make an appointment as soon as your condition impacts on your ability to study.

If you have a continuing condition you should contact Student Services to arrange a Student Access Plan(Disability) [SAPD]. You must still submit the application form

Non-permissible circumstances

Extensions will not be granted where the School is not satisfied you took reasonable measures to avoid the circumstances that contributed to you not submitting by the due date. The following are not grounds for an extension:

holiday arrangements (including overseas travel);

misreading a due date;

social and leisure events;

moving house;

pressure of work/competing deadlines;

computer issues.

The above late submission information does not apply to honours theses, 8 unit research theses, 4 unit research projects and 2 unit research reports. For extension and late submission information in relation to these courses see section 5.4 of the course profile.

The due dates for assignments must be adhered to and you are reminded to keep a copy of your work, in case of lost assignments or disputes over grading.

You will incur penalties if your work is submitted late (i.e. after the due date and without an approved extension).

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue. The penalty once calculated is deducted from the marks awarded for the assessment. Assessment submitted more than 10 days after the due date will receive zero marks.
other_assessment_information:
The assessment for this course comprises 50% team effort building a case study for innovation capability and 50% individual effort to develop & test your analytical skills through your short/extended answer quiz responses and capture your personal reflection in your Research Journal, as outlined in section 5.1 above.

ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise advised by your course coordinator, all written assignments are to be electronically submitted using Blackboard (Learn) https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . The instructions for submission below can also be found on the "Assessment" link in Blackboard. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this eCP.

IMPORTANT: Online Submission

Ensure you receive a Turnitin submission ID or Learn.UQ confirmation number, and retain this number as proof of submission. If you do not receive proof of submission, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful and take steps to address the issue. It is your responsibility to ensure the assignment has been successfully submitted. Any requests to waive the late submission penalty without proof of submission will not be considered under any circumstances.

The method of submission will be decided by your Course Coordinator, either Turnitin or Blackboard. Upload your assignment using the relevant link created for your use .

INSTRUCTIONS FOR SUBMITTING YOUR ASSIGNMENT & CONFIRMING YOUR SUBMISSION

TURNITIN

Submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/0#0

Upload your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/1#1

Review and submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/2#2

Confirm your submission - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/3#3

BLACKBOARD

Access your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/learnuq-assessment/blackboard-assignments/assignment-submission-instructions

Submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/1#1

Add comments - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/2#2

Submit - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/3#3

Review/resubmit - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/4#4

For individual and/or Team assessment items:

The assignment must be submitted electronically using the online submission link located in the Blackboard Assessment menu https://learn.uq.edu.au/ .

FOR TEAM ASSIGNMENTS

All team assignments must have ONE nominated representative's details at the top of the coversheet, with details of other team members listed on the lower section.

Students should keep a list of their team members' full names and contact details.

Please check with your course coordinator if online submission is required for a team assignment.

For team assessment items:

Teams are self-selected towards developing a case study through the semester, delivering this project for grading through presentations and a written submission, in accordance with the Assessment Summary table in Section 5.1 above (* indicates team assessment item) . The team presentations and case study must have ONE nominated representative's details (the Team Captain ) at the top of the opening slide or coversheet, with details of other team members listed on the lower section. Team Captains should keep a list of their team members' full names and contact details for contact throughout the semester.

A key component for each of these team items is a peer assessment by each team member against four criteria. The written project plan must be submitted electronically through the Blackboard Assessment link using the Turn-It-In icon.

NB: Team submission is NOT complete until each team member has completed the peer assessment, submitted electronically through the Blackboard Assessment link.

Hardcopy submissions are only required for team assessment items that are difficult to upload to the course Blackboard site, for example posters and multi-modal assessment tasks. If the Course Co-ordinator has requested any hardcopy submission, please note the following:

Hardcopy assignments must be lodged by the due date and time stated in the course profile, usually submitted through UQBS/BEL Reception, level 2 Colin Clark Building (39).

Each assignment must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed Cover Sheet.

All such hardcopy submissions will be stamped with the date and time of receipt, or if submitted after hours with the date of the NEXT business day.

Students must sign the cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work.

Students must produce their student ID card (or driver's license) to collect marked assignments or hardcopy components.

Incomplete or incorrect assignment coversheets can cause delays in processing your assignment.

Your Course Co-ordinator wil advise you when any hardcopy submissions are ready for collection.

Assignments should not be emailed to the Course Co-ordinator unless specifically requested.

PEER ASSESSMENT

Submission of each team assessment item outlined in the Assessment Summary table of Section 5.1 occurs in two parts: firstly, the team presentation delivery or report upload by the Team Captain as a team component;and, secondly, the completion of the online peer assessment to generate a peer assessment factor (PAF) for each member of your team as an individual component. The PAF moderates the team score by yielding an individual score for each of the team assessment items, by each student distributing 100 points across all team members against each of four criteria:

overall impact on team;

enabling team function & problem solving;

novel idea generation; and

adaptive & persistent throughout the project

Comments against each allocation of points you make, for each member against each criterion, are not compulsory but strongly encouraged, particularly if your score singles out positively or negatively distinctive conduct by any individual. These scores are calculated by Blackboard to generate a single PAF, with 1.00 being the normative value, which is then applied to the team assignment/presentation score to yield an individual mark for that assessment item. For further description of PAF and its moderating interpretation, refer to: https://www.elearning.uq.edu.au/content/paf-formula-and-moderation-overview

NB: Team assessments are not complete until both parts have been submitted for each team assessment item. Late penalties in accordance with Section 5.3 will be applied for the whole marks available for each item if either part is submitted after the due time & date, as shown in the Assessment Summary table in Section 5.1 above.

COLLECTION OF ASSIGNMENTS

Responses to assignments will usually be delivered online. Your course coordinator will advise when any hardcopy items are ready for collection and from where the assignments can be collected. Note: You must produce your student ID card (or driver's license) when collecting in person.

REFERENCING AND CITING

Assignments must be substantially your own work. If you wish to report another author's point of view you should do so in your own words, and properly footnote the reference in accordance with the school style. Direct quotations should be used sparingly, form a small part of your work, and must be placed in quotation marks and referenced.

Any material taken from texts and other references, including electronic resources, CD-ROMS, and the Internet, must be acknowledged using the accepted School style.

Students are encouraged to discuss issues that arise in this course together, however, the written work you submit must be entirely your own . Similarly, you must not help another student to cheat by lending assignments (present or past).

For more information on referencing styles, visit the library or see: https://web.library.uq.edu.au/research-tools-techniques/referencing/referencing-style-guides .

If you do not reference correctly, you could be found guilty of academic misconduct. Please see this link for more information: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/student-integrity-and-conduct/academic-integrity-and-student-conduct.

For more information on assessment, please review the UQ Assessment Policy at https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment

DEFERRED EXAMS

Midsemester Examinations

A student who fails to sit the midsemester examination and/or the quizzes at the scheduled times will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the School receives a deferred request on Si-net with appropriate documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence. The application must be submitted either prior to the scheduled date of the midsemester exam, or no later than five days after the midsemester examination. All deferred midsemester examinations within UQ Business School will take place on Saturday 6th October 2018 . A timetable for the deferred midsemester examinations will be available early in the week commencing 2nd October 2018 .

This will be the only opportunity to take the deferred midsemester examination. Any student who is granted a deferred mid-semester examination and who fails to attend at the appointed time and place will be awarded a mark of zero as per the PPL rules. There is no opportunity to defer a deferred examination under any circumstances.

The process for applying for deferred midsemester exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred midsemester examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests .

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

Be aware of Discretionary exam request – if you use the discretionary deferred option, it will NOT be available to you again.

If you are applying on medical grounds or other eligible circumstances DO NOT use the Discretionary option.

The timetable will be found on the UQ Business School webpage > Student > Student Notices > under Deferred Midsemester Examination (see http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student-notices )

Students who have 3 or more deferred exams will be notified by the Examinations Section to put in an Alternative Arrangements form.

Students who have 2 deferred exams will have a choice of when to sit both exams.

Any enquiries regarding midsemester deferred examinations should be directed to the Assessment, Examinations and Misconducts Coordinator, exams.officer@business.uq.edu.au .

Saturday midsemester examination dates:

Saturday 1 September 2018

Saturday 8 September 2018

Saturday 15 September 2018

Final Examinations

A student who is unable to sit a scheduled final examination must apply for a deferred examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held.

The process for applying for deferred final exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred midsemester examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests .

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

The Examination Section and Faculties may approve applications for deferred examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Deferred examinations are not automatically granted. For further information (such as evidence required and time limits) please see My.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/ .

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

Supplementary assessment is an additional opportunity to demonstrate that the learning requirements for the course have been satisfied and that the graduate attributes for the course have been attained. Supplementary assessment will be granted only where the Program Rules and the Supplementary Assessment Procedures allow.

For further information on supplementary assessment please see My.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/ .
